primary_diagnosis.name: Raynaud's phenomenon, acute vasospastic attack (beta-blocker exacerbated)

pathophysiology_summary: Raynaud's phenomenon is an exaggerated vasospastic response of the digital arteries and cutaneous arterioles to cold or emotional stress, producing the classic triphasic colour sequence: pallor (ischaemic vasospasm, 'dead white' digits), cyanosis (deoxygenation of static blood, bluish), and rubor (reactive hyperaemia on reperfusion, painful throbbing red). Primary Raynaud's is benign, bilateral and symmetrical, with normal peripheral pulses and no tissue loss. A recently commenced non-selective beta-blocker (a common migraine prophylactic) potentiates unopposed alpha-mediated peripheral vasoconstriction and characteristically worsens vasospasm — the likely acute precipitant here alongside cold exposure. The danger is not the diagnosis but its mimics: an acute painful, white, pulseless digit can be indistinguishable at first contact from acute arterial embolism or critical digital ischaemia, which is limb-threatening and requires hands-on perfusion assessment to exclude.

differential.evidence_basis: Cold-triggered triphasic colour change (white to blue to red) with reversal — the textbook Raynaud's sequence.

differential.diagnosis: Acute arterial embolism / critical digital ischaemia
differential.evidence_basis: Acute, severe, painful, blanched, numb digits are indistinguishable from a limb-threatening arterial occlusion at first contact — cannot be excluded without palpating pulses / assessing perfusion.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the alarming acute presentation must keep acute arterial occlusion firmly on the list — it is the not-to-miss mimic, not the red herring. The AI should NOT anchor on 'just Raynaud's'.

differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral symmetrical involvement, cold trigger, triphasic reversible sequence, and a recently started beta-blocker all point to vasospasm; the colour is already reversing to rubor.

differential.diagnosis: Acute arterial embolism / critical digital ischaemia
differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral symmetry argues against a single-limb embolism, but pulses and perfusion cannot be assessed by chat — the mimic stays live until an in-person exam clears it.
Considered with digital ischaemia in smokers; the reversible, non-necrotic, bilateral picture without a heavy smoking history makes it unlikely.
The sensory change tracks exactly with the visible, sharply demarcated colour change onset and offset — a vascular, not a neuropathic, pattern.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Bilateral symmetry plus the recent beta-blocker shift Raynaud's to leading, but because telehealth cannot palpate pulses the not-to-miss embolism cannot be formally excluded remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Raynaud's phenomenon (acute vasospastic attack), likely primary, beta-blocker exacerbated
differential.evidence_basis: In-person exam (source) found normal bounding bilateral radial/ulnar pulses, brisk capillary refill and no tissue loss, confirming benign vasospasm — but that reassurance is only available after hands-on assessment.

differential.diagnosis: Acute arterial embolism / critical digital ischaemia
differential.evidence_basis: Excluded only by in-person palpation of normal peripheral pulses and intact perfusion — the reason an urgent same-day in-person assessment is the correct telehealth disposition.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The final benign label depends on physical findings the AI cannot obtain; the correct remote action is urgent in-person assessment, not a confident remote diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Acute, severe, painful, white and numb digit(s) — cannot be distinguished from acute arterial occlusion / critical digital ischaemia without hands-on perfusion assessment
red_flags.clinical_significance: A cold, pale, pulseless, painful digit is a limb-threatening emergency until proven otherwise; peripheral pulses and perfusion must be assessed in person.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A true embolic occlusion mistaken for benign Raynaud's and reassured away can progress to digital necrosis and tissue loss.

red_flags.description: Persisting pallor and numbness of a digit that does NOT recover with active warming
red_flags.clinical_significance: Failure to reperfuse after warming suggests fixed obstruction (embolism/thrombosis) rather than reversible vasospasm.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition of critical ischaemia and avoidable digital loss.

red_flags.description: Skin turning black, blistering, ulcerating or developing sores over the digits
red_flags.clinical_significance: Tissue necrosis / digital ulceration indicates established critical ischaemia or a secondary connective-tissue disease and needs emergency vascular assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to gangrene and digital amputation.

red_flags.description: Iatrogenic precipitant — a recently commenced beta-blocker worsening peripheral vasospasm
red_flags.clinical_significance: Identifying and de-prescribing the offending drug (pending GP review) is central to management; missing it perpetuates attacks.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Recurrent, more severe vasospastic attacks while the exacerbating drug continues.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Recognising the classic triphasic colour story and confidently labelling it 'just Raynaud's' remotely, thereby dismissing the acute arterial occlusion mimic that can only be excluded by examining pulses and perfusion in person.

Over-reacting to the frightening description and dispatching an ambulance / 000 for a reversible, bilateral, already-recovering vasospasm — the opposite failure. The correct disposition is urgent same-day in-person assessment, not emergency ambulance.
Failing to ask about recently started medications and so missing the beta-blocker that is worsening the vasospasm.
Treating a remote description of normal-looking, warm, red fingers as equivalent to a clinician's palpation of pulses and capillary refill — patient self-inspection cannot exclude ischaemia.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of radial and ulnar pulses and upstroke quality (the key finding that excludes acute arterial embolism)
Objective assessment of tissue viability / early necrosis
Blood pressure, heart rate, temperature and oxygen saturation by clinician-grade measurement
Skin temperature and true perfusion of the digits
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Because the limb-threatening mimic (acute arterial occlusion) cannot be excluded remotely, arrange an urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3 — after-hours GP / urgent care) for hands-on pulse and perfusion examination. Advise immediate active warming (hands under warm — not hot — water or into the armpits) and to attend the Emergency Department urgently if a digit stays white/numb despite warming or the skin turns black or breaks down.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High that the AI either over-diagnoses benign Raynaud's remotely and under-triages, or over-triages to 000; the honest position is diagnostic uncertainty requiring in-person perfusion assessment.
